posts.alt_text: Photo by AnOther Magazine on May 29, 2026. May be a graphic of magazine, poster and text that says 'AnOther "I Want to Celebrate the Great Things About Fashion and Culture" SUSANNAH FRANKEL IN CONVERSATION WITH HUBE MAGAZINE'.

posts.caption:
“Wondering is underestimated,” @susannahfrankel tells @hube.magazine’s @alekskovaleva. ⁠
⁠
As editor-in-chief of AnOther Magazine for the past decade, Frankel has guided the publication into a new era. In conversation, she reflects on a career spanning Blitz, The Guardian and The Independent, before arriving at AnOther in 2001. Discussing the designers, photographers and collaborators who shaped her perspective – from Lee Alexander McQueen and Hussein Chalayan to Rei Kawakubo and Karl Lagerfeld – Frankel also considers the realities of independent publishing and the changing role of criticism today.⁠
⁠
“AnOther is not only a fashion magazine. It is built around culture more broadly,” Frankel tells hube. “But fashion, in a cultural context, is at its core. For me, it has always been about the designers.”⁠
⁠
Hube’s interview with this magazine’s editor-in-chief at the link in bio 📲
posts.alt_text: Photo by AnOther Magazine on May 30, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, poster and text that says 'AnOther Clarissa: Virginia Woolf's Modernist Classic Mrs Dalloway Comes to Nigeria'.

posts.caption:
Winking to 2016’s formative fashion moments and a childhood as a competitive freestyle disco dancer, @mafcsm student @oliverjeanes’s second collection finds beauty in bad taste. ⁠
⁠
“[Freestyle disco dancing] is mental,” he tells @viiolethayward. “Eight girls and I would go on the floor in the most insane, over-the-top rhinestone costumes you’ve ever seen in your life and a questionable amount of fake tan. You’d go, throw yourself around the floor, and hope that one of the judges standing around would notice you.” ⁠
⁠
Designed ahead of starting his MA at Central Saint Martins, Jeanes’ second collection builds on his penchant for ironesty, and this time, his experience as a dancer is in the spotlight. Read more at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
Photography: @zacharielewertoff. Creative consultant: @darrianagrande. Casting: @laurenvanmeeuwen. Hair: @mthr0905. Make-up: @_mahomoriyama_. Set design: @2cult. Lighting assistant: @caeldevers. Photography assistant: @samuelcgurung. Styling assistant: @minnadebagota

posts.caption:
@cary.kwok is known for playful erotic paintings and sculptures featuring towering phallic forms and profusions of glistening ejaculate. His works often combine human-made objects with the body, touching on everything from furniture design to fashion illustration and speculative architecture.⁠
⁠
For a new exhibition at @sessionsartsclub, the Hong Kong-born, London-based artist presents new works inspired by the visual language of the 1980s, drawing on interior design, cinematography, music videos, advertisements and fashion editorials. Executed in acrylic and ink on thick paper, the paintings are tender and charged with emotional possibility. ⁠
⁠
The paintings can be seen by anyone dining at Sessions Arts Club or by appointment now. Read an interview with the artist at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Artworks by @cary.kwok. Courtesy of @sessionsartsclub

posts.caption:
5,783 contact sheets spanning Peter Hujar’s photographic life are stored in eight banker boxes. Each sheet is encased in a plastic wallet, separated, and sorted vertically like documents in a filing cabinet. ⁠
⁠
There is nothing especially remarkable about these prosaic boxes: colourless, functional, quietly banal. They could easily be passed over without a second glance. Yet inside, they hold an entire world. On the occasion of Hujar: Contact, a new book published by @mack_publishing alongside an exhibition of the same name at @themorganlibrary and Museum, curator Joel Smith and archivist @olivia.mc speak to both the artistic and personal intimacy of Hujar’s contact sheets.⁠
⁠
“Nothing that supersedes the intimacy of the contact sheets themselves,” McCall tells @w.lliam. Read the interview at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Hujar: Contact. Courtesy of MACK; Fraenkel Gallery, San Francisco; and Ortuzar, New York. © The Peter Hujar Archive / Artists Rights Society (ARS)⁠
1. Peter Hujar, contact prints, David Wojnarowicz, 1981, from Hujar: Contact by Joel Smith (MACK and The Morgan Library & Museum, 2026)⁠
2. Peter Hujar, contact prints, Susan Sontag at home, 1975, from Hujar: Contact by Joel Smith (MACK and The Morgan Library & Museum, 2026)⁠
3. Peter Hujar, contact prints, Jackie Curtis at 10 East 23rd Street, 1970, from Hujar: Contact by Joel Smith (MACK and The Morgan Library & Museum, 2026)⁠
5. Peter Hujar, contact prints, self-portraits at 189 Second Avenue, 1974, from Hujar: Contact by Joel Smith (MACK and The Morgan Library & Museum, 2026)⁠
6. Portraits of Hujar from 1957, 1959, 1960, 1971, and 1976, contact prints, from Hujar: Contact by Joel Smith (MACK and The Morgan Library & Museum, 2026)⁠
7. Peter Hujar photographed by Arielle Dombasle, contact print, 189 Second Avenue, 1983, from Hujar: Contact by Joel Smith (MACK and The Morgan Library & Museum, 2026)⁠
⁠
@peterhujararchive #PeterHujar⁠

posts.caption:
@nevengallery presents ‘You’re only happy when you can see something die!’ A group exhibition curated by @leocostelloe, featuring five artists: @greer_lankton_archives_museum, @ki.yoong, @j.c.mccormack, @olliejailer, #tiinavanhatupa, @_louisthompson.
From works that quietly stage silk tulips and peonies arranged in vases and sealed in plastic, to pieces that compress the icon of Marilyn Monroe to its extreme in order to expose the endless reproduction and consumption of images, the exhibition moves across a range of approaches.
This exhibition captures the moment when we try to preserve beauty forever by sealing it away, and how that act, paradoxically, takes away its life and turns it into something preserved and lifeless. The space is small, yet the density of meaning it holds feels heavier than that of most exhibitions.
📌Exhibition Details
• Date: 29 May - 18 July
• Location: N 353, Cambridge Heath Roan, E2 9RA
Words by Julie Jeong
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷 @a2z_fashionmag

posts.caption:
On 29 May, a new photo of a Dior bag was uploaded to Jonathan Anderson’s personal Instagram. The artwork featuring a figure with blonde hair in an all-black look immediately caught the eye, but who is the artist newly highlighted by Jonathan Anderson, who is well known for his deep appreciation of art?
The artwork featured on the bag is by Eunnam Hong, a Korean artist based in New York. In an interview with Vogue Korea, she describes fashion as something far beyond mere style. “Fashion is fascinating because it transforms things that cannot be touched into something that can be embodied and felt. In my paintings, fashion serves as a tool — almost like a psychological game — to express present emotions or critical perspectives.”
Eunnam, who often wears leather jackets from Schott, also treats the clothing of her figures as a form of language. “I choose outfits based on the characters I create. Ideas related to my work often emerge in interesting ways, and I find them reflected in the everyday expressions of the people in my paintings.” Swipe to explore Eunnam Hong’s work.
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷 jonathan.anderson, @eunnamhong
@dior #Dior #JonathanAnderson #EunnamHong

posts.caption:
In an industry where surnames are inherited currencies, Bazaar India’s April-May cover star, Tabu (@tabutiful), has operated for over three decades on a mononym. To understand her career is to understand what it meant to say yes to things the industry was not ready for and to have the instinct to recognise those things as worthy, even when no template existed for them. But she refuses to call it rebellion. “It was simply a reflection of who I am. The roles I chose, the decisions I made—they came from some similar place throughout my life. Some part of me has been completely unchanged,” she insists.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
Editor: Rasna Bhasin (@rasnabhasin)
Interview: Malika Halder (@malikahalder)
Photographer: Soujit Das (@soujit.das)
Stylist: Gopalika Virmani (@gopalikavirmani)

posts.caption:
Marlo Kelly is on the cusp of bona fide international stardom. The angel-faced Australian actor stars in the Emmy Award-nominated sci-fi series #3BodyProblem, and speaks to BAZAAR from Budapest, where she is preparing to start shooting season three. From a school production of ‘Macbeth’ and ‘Home and Away’ to her current role as “the scary lady from the show”, Kelly recounts the humble – and serendipitous – start of her acting career. “It feels like an idea that I was born with,” shares the self-described “theatre nerd”.
Through the link in bio, see @marlokelly photographed back home in Australia and continue reading the digital cover story.
Marlo Kelly wears @ralphlauren and @poloralphlauren on the June 2026 digital cover of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia/New Zealand.

posts.caption:
“It is the luckiest thing that’s ever happened in my life,” says @marlokelly of the moment a talent agent signed her following her performance as Lady Macbeth in a school production. Upending the usual trope, the actor’s parents were supportive of her prioritising art over academics, on occasion. “One time [in year nine], I was worried about exams as well as a play,” she recalls. “And [my dad] was like, ‘don’t worry about your exams – just learn your lines.”
At the link in bio, continue reading BAZAAR’s interview with the #3BodyProblem star.

posts.caption:
Carlos Nazario (@mr_carlos_nazario) has helped redefine how fashion media expresses subculture in a luxury context, making history along the way as the first Black editor to style a cover for American Vogue.
But he grew up in Queens, New York, in a big Puerto Rican family with no connections to fashion. His grandmother Efna was his earliest influence — a woman who understood, intuitively, the power of how you present yourself to the world and shared a key lesson with him.
That lesson has guided his journey as he left for Paris as a teenager, came home, worked his way through internships at W magazine and Love in London, and spent seven years as first assistant to stylist Joe McKenna. When he went out on his own, he built a creative world that looked like the one he’d grown up in — and started making images that put it at the centre of fashion.
This week on the BoF Podcast, @ImranAmed talks to Nazario about the nightlife scene that shaped his creative identity, what it cost to break into an industry that wasn’t built for someone like him, and why the pictures that endure are the ones made with heart.
🎙️ Listen now #linkinbio #TheBoFPodcast
📷 Getty Images

posts.caption:
In 2026, the luxury fashion industry has been placing bets, disproportionately and deliberately, on women athletes.
The relationship between elite female athletes and the fashion and beauty industries is not new: Zheng Qinwen is a Dior ambassador; Iga Swiatek carries Lancôme. But the architecture of these relationships is changing fast, and in one direction only.
The question becomes: what are those athletes being offered in return?
Olivier Rivard-Cohen argues the framework that turns visibility into brand ownership for top female athletes is almost entirely unbuilt. Read his full opinion piece at the #linkinbio
📷 angel.reese

posts.caption:
Gucci’s reboot under designer Demna is going in the right direction, Kering CEO Luca de Meo told shareholders at the French group’s annual general meeting on Thursday.
It’s been no more than six weeks since the executive laid out his plans to turn the French group around at a capital markets day in mid-April, but the clock is ticking for de Meo to show results after years of declining sales at Kering’s flagship brand.
BoF’s Mimosa Spencer (@mimosaspencer) explores whether early momentum around Demna’s vision can translate into a broader turnaround at Gucci. Read more #linkinbio
📷 @gucci

posts.caption:
Mass-market and premium brands alike are pushing upmarket — it’s a playbook brands across price ranges are employing today. Gap and Uniqlo hired Zac Posen and former Givenchy designer Claire Waight Keller, respectively; Cos and H&M staged fashion shows at New York and London fashion weeks.
As brands take pages out of the luxury playbook, there’s new pressure on the brands at the top of the fashion pyramid. Luxury labels have to find new ways to emphasise their elevated positioning.
BoF's Joan Kennedy (@joooankennedyyy) explores how luxury brands are sharpening their point of view to emphasise their elevated positioning. Read more #linkinbio
📷 @hm, @mango

posts.caption:
Maison Lanvin has appointed Barbara Werschine as chief executive officer, the latest step in a broader effort to stabilise and relaunch the historic French luxury house following a period of operational and financial strain.
Werschine, who started in the role on May 26, will be responsible for steering Lanvin’s global strategy, accelerating its growth and reinforcing its positioning internationally, Lanvin said on Thursday.
“My vision is clear: to sustainably rebuild the house’s desirability and core fundamentals,” Werschine said in a statement.
Read more on the maison’s latest appointment #linkinbio
✍️ @ericsylvers
📷 Spotlight/Launchmetrics.com

posts.caption:
When photographer Greg Brennan spotted Kate Moss in a fur coat at the bottom of a fire exit, cigarette in hand, he knew he'd stumbled across something special.
The year was 2007, and what he didn't realize is that one of the resulting photos — not even his favorite from the night — would become an emblem of the supermodel's "party era" and the best-known image of his near-four-decade career.
The mid-aughts photo's enduring appeal is, partly, its mundanity. In that quiet, unguarded moment, Moss was just like any other 30-something having a night out on the town.
And yet she is perhaps the only person who could appear that put-together while being ambushed in a stairwell. "It's kind of a mixture between a ballerina and Janis Joplin," Brennan said. "It's very rock 'n' roll."
Not all was as it seemed, though.
📸 : Greg Brennan
posts.alt_text:
Sue Tilley, the 60-something retired benefits supervisor and subject of British artist Lucian Freud's monumental painting "Sleeping by the Lion Carpet" (1996), has traveled from her home on the south coast of England to see the work before it heads to auction next month. The portrait is estimated to fetch between £25-35 million ($33-45M) at the Lewis Collection sale on June 24. But how does it feel to have your body up for auction for tens millions of dollars? We asked her.

Tap our link in bio to read more.

posts.caption:
When Icelandic Chinese musician Laufey dropped her latest music video last month, starring a cast of mixed white and Asian celebrities, the internet went wild.
"Laufey collecting wasians like pokemon," read one social media comment, using a portmanteau to describe people of mixed white and Asian descent. "WASIAN AVENGERS," another comment cheered.
She is among those who have publicly embraced the Wasian label — which has helped many people who self-identify as such to feel more seen.
This heightened attention coincides with a growing fascination in the West around Asian entertainment and culture (think the Netflix smash hit "KPop Demon Hunters," the recent Chinamaxxing trend, K-beauty products, and even Labubus). Increased visibility has crossed over into the real world, with "Wasian meetups" taking place earlier this May in San Francisco and New York City.
For some, it was a powerful and positive experience to see themselves represented and to connect with others.
But the meetups have also triggered a heated debate about race and identity that has reached a fever pitch online in recent weeks. The controversy has raised questions about who is represented, and why — as well as what it means to be multiracial in America.
Tap the link in @cnn’s bio for more.
📸 Nicole Mago/Warren Fu/Quentin Nguyen-duy
📹 Quentin Nguyen-duy

Sue Tilley, the 60-something retired benefits supervisor and subject of British artist Lucian Freud's monumental painting "Sleeping by the Lion Carpet" (1996), has traveled from her home on the south coast of England to see the work before it heads to auction next month. posts.caption:
When she was four, the artist Widline Cadet (@widline_ ) was separated from her mother for six years as she emigrated from Haiti to New York to pursue a better life for her family.
At 10 years old, Cadet reunited with her mother in New York, but as she grew into adulthood, she realized that she didn't know her well at all. Nor did Cadet have a larger sense of her family, the ancestral threads that weave back through time.
Photographs of her own childhood and family were scarce. Her mother didn't have a picture of her own mother. Memories faded with each passing year.
For nearly a decade, Cadet has been crafting her own "living archive," exploring the diasporic experience and the elusiveness of memory. The largest presentation of her work to date is on view at @milwaukeeart.
📸: Widline Cadet

posts.caption:
Fashion has spent the last twenty years trying to recreate what Carolyn Bessette-Kennedy did naturally.
There was nothing complicated about the way she dressed. No obvious trends, no attention-grabbing styling tricks, no need to constantly reinvent herself. Yet somehow, a white shirt, a black skirt or a simple slip dress looked more elegant on her than most red carpet looks ever could.
That’s probably why people are still obsessed with her. Her outfits felt real. They weren’t built for social media, they weren’t chasing relevance, and they never looked overthought. She simply understood what worked for her and stuck to it.
More than two decades later, fashion still keeps returning to the same formula: clean silhouettes, understated luxury and effortless confidence. In many ways, we’re all still chasing the kind of elegance Carolyn made look so easy.
All images courtesy of Getty Images.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 29, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, makeup, magazine and text that says 'Madonna's All Time Most Iconic Outfits. CULT'.

posts.caption:
Few artists have used fashion as fearlessly as Madonna. Throughout the 1980s, 90s and early 2000s, she constantly reinvented herself, turning clothing into one of the most powerful tools of her identity. From the lace gloves and crucifixes of her early years to Jean Paul Gaultier’s iconic cone bra, every era came with a new image that would go on to influence an entire generation.
Her looks challenged ideas of femininity, sexuality, religion and celebrity culture long before it became common in pop music. Decades later, many of her most iconic outfits still feel surprisingly modern because they were never just about trends. They were about creating a character, starting a conversation and pushing boundaries.
All images courtesy of Getty Images.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 30, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, blonde hair, people standing, magazine and text that says 'The Woman Who Defined 90s Elegance. CULT'.

posts.caption:
Victoria Beckham’s Resort 2027 collection is beautiful but a little boring. The tailoring is sharp, the fabrics look expensive and the silhouettes have that very „controlled“ elegance her brand has become known for over the years. Long fluid dresses, clean suiting, muted tones and soft draping create a wardrobe that feels wearable.
But at the same time, that’s also the problem with the collection: it feels almost too controlled. Everything is aesthetically pleasing, yet very little feels emotionally exciting or surprising. The clothes are tasteful in the safest possible way. Looking through the collection, you keep waiting for a moment of tension, a strange silhouette, an unexpected styling decision or something that disrupts the perfection a little, but it never really comes.
That’s what makes the collection feel slightly forgettable despite how refined it is. Modern luxury fashion has become so obsessed with looking elegant, expensive and “timeless” that many collections now end up blending together visually. Victoria Beckham understands restraint very well, but sometimes restraint without contrast can become boring.
And honestly, that’s what this collection represents perfectly: fashion that looks beautiful on Instagram, beautiful in motion and beautiful in showrooms, but rarely creates the kind of emotion or personality that makes people truly obsessed with it.
Collection Credits
Collection — Victoria Beckham Resort 2027
Designer — Victoria Beckham
Brand — Victoria Beckham
Presentation — Resort 2027 Collection
Source — Vogue Runway Collection Review & Slideshow
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 30, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, blonde hair, people standing, magazine and text that says 'The Woman Who Defined 90s Elegance. CULT'.

posts.caption:
DSquared2 SS2010 was pure excess in the best possible way. Dean and Dan Caten built the show around hyper-sexual glamour, distressed denim, tiny shorts, low-rise silhouettes, oversized belts, body-hugging dresses and chaotic layering that felt somewhere between backstage rockstar culture and early 2000s paparazzi fashion. Nothing about the collection was subtle. Every look pushed this very unapologetic idea of confidence, sex appeal and nightlife energy that defined so much of fashion during that era.
What made the collection stand out was how aggressively styled everything felt. The ripped denim, heavy accessories, bronzed skin, towering heels and exaggerated silhouettes created characters rather than just outfits. DSquared2 understood fashion as spectacle and attitude first, which is probably why these runway shows still feel memorable years later.
Looking back now, the collection feels surprisingly relevant again. Fashion has slowly moved away from overly polished “clean girl” dressing and back toward messier, more chaotic styling. Tiny denim shorts, layered belts, washed fabrics, revealing silhouettes and that whole “slightly trashy but glamorous” aesthetic are everywhere again this summer. A lot of what DSquared2 was doing in 2010 suddenly feels very connected to the current obsession with indie sleaze, Y2K paparazzi fashion and imperfect styling.
What once looked excessive now almost feels current again.
Collection Credits
Collection — DSquared2 Spring/Summer 2010 Ready-to-Wear
Designers — Dean Caten & Dan Caten
Brand — Dsquared2
Presentation — Milan Fashion Week Spring/Summer 2010
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 30, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, blonde hair, people standing, magazine and text that says 'The Woman Who Defined 90s Elegance. CULT'.

posts.caption:
In “Dream your paintings, paint your dreams!” Tim Walker turns fashion into a surreal art installation inspired by the distorted figures and emotional intensity of Egon Schiele. The editorial feels less like a traditional fashion story and more like stepping inside a painting that’s constantly shifting between fantasy, performance and psychological theatre.
What makes Tim Walker’s work so recognizable is the way he approaches fashion photography almost like world building. The clothes are important, but atmosphere always comes first. In this story, twisted body language, exaggerated silhouettes and theatrical staging create images that feel strange, playful and slightly unsettling at the same time. Walker has always understood that the strongest editorials are the ones that completely transport you somewhere else instead of simply showing beautiful clothing.
The Schiele inspiration works especially well because the editorial never tries to imitate fine art too literally. Instead, it translates Expressionism into fashion imagery through movement, distortion and emotion. Kiki Willems and James Crewe almost look like living brushstrokes inside the set, while Jacob K’s styling pushes the silhouettes further into this surreal, off-balanced world. (fashionCOW￼)
What also makes the story stand out years later is how physical and handmade everything feels. In a time where so much fashion imagery becomes overly retouched or digitally polished, Tim Walker’s editorials still feel tactile, theatrical and deeply human. That’s probably why his work continues to feel timeless while so many campaigns disappear after one season.
Editorial Credits
Editorial — “Dream your paintings, paint your dreams!”
Publication — i-D Magazine Spring/Summer 2017
Photography — Tim Walker
Styling — Jacob K
Models — Kiki Willems, James Crewe
Hair — Shon
Make-up — Sam Bryant
Set Design — Emma Roach
Casting Director — Madeleine Østlie
Production — Jeffrey Delich
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 30, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, blonde hair, people standing, magazine and text that says 'The Woman Who Defined 90s Elegance. CULT'.

posts.caption:
What makes this British Vogue story so powerful is the emotional weight behind every image. Editorials often try to imitate intimacy, but this one feels real. You can immediately sense the connection between Anok Yai and her mother, Nyibol Akuei, without the images needing to explain themselves. There’s a softness, protection and familiarity in the way they move around each other that makes the story feel much bigger than a normal fashion editorial.
Anok has spoken many times about how close she is to her mother, especially after her recent health struggles. In the Vogue interview, Nyibol describes how painful it was seeing her daughter in the hospital and how deeply connected they are as a family. That emotional closeness completely changes the energy of the editorial. It stops feeling like “fashion images” and starts feeling archival — almost like family history documented through luxury fashion photography. (British Vogue￼)
Visually, the editorial works because Rafael Pavarotti understands how to photograph Black skin with depth, richness and drama without flattening it into something overly polished. The lighting feels cinematic and spiritual at the same time. Every frame has scale, but the relationship between mother and daughter always remains the center of attention. That balance is incredibly difficult to achieve in fashion photography.
Anok Yai has always had one of the strongest presences in modern modeling because she understands narrative. She doesn’t just model clothes — she creates atmosphere. And here, that atmosphere feels deeply personal. The editorial quietly speaks about migration, family, survival, beauty and legacy without becoming performative or overexplained. That’s what separates a great editorial from a forgettable campaign: it leaves behind emotion, not just visuals.
Publication — British Vogue
Model — Anok Yai
Featuring — Nyibol Akuei
Photography — Rafael Pavarotti
Styling / Fashion Editor — Kate Phelan
Interview — Funmi Fetto
Creative Direction — British Vogue / Condé Nast creative team
Hair — Eugene Souleiman
Make-up — Hiromi Ueda
Set Design — Ibby Njoya
Production — Prodn Paris

posts.caption:
Young New Yorkers could be among those hardest hit by new SNAP work requirements.⁠
⁠
Following the passage of President Trump’s Big Beautiful Bill in July 2025, new restrictions have been added to SNAP, the programme that helps people on low incomes buy food. Now, many recipients must demonstrate that they work, volunteer or attend school for at least 80 hours a month.⁠
⁠
The new requirements came into effect on March 1, but will begin to be felt by New Yorkers as early as June 1. Even though the deadline is approaching quickly, there is still time to act.⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
For decades, fine jewellery innovation has been driven by design, craftsmanship and exceptional gemstones.
But what happens when engineering enters the equation?
At JCK Las Vegas 2026, Jaipur-based manufacturer @brijjewelsllc will introduce its D Standard collection — combining GIA-certified natural diamonds with Nitinol, a shape-memory alloy more commonly associated with aerospace and advanced engineering applications.
The result is what Brij calls “Engineered Luxury” — where innovation, wearability and natural diamond craftsmanship intersect.
The bigger question for the industry:
💬 Could advanced materials become the next major frontier in high jewellery?
Share your thoughts below.
#DiamondWorld #NaturalDiamonds #JCKLasVegas #JewelleryInnovation

#DesignerSpotlight @by_couture @francescavillajewelry

Casino chips. Vintage cameos. Antique treasures. Even beloved pets.

Which detail surprised you the most?

👇 Tell us below.

posts.caption:
Africa supplies most of the world’s natural diamonds, but captures only a small portion of the value.
In an insightful conversation with Diamond World, Wellington Mpandi, Vice President of the South African Young Diamond Beneficiators’ Guild, outlines why producer nations must move beyond rough exports and build stronger domestic value chains.
Key Takeaways for the Diamond Industry:
Diamonds are economic engines for African nations not just luxury products.
Natural diamonds must be differentiated through origin, rarity, and human stories.
Beneficiation (cutting, polishing, grading, and manufacturing) is critical for long-term resilience.
Traceability and responsible sourcing are becoming premium drivers.
Producer nations need a stronger voice in global category marketing.
The Big Industry Lesson:
The future of natural diamonds will be shaped by those who create value at the source and communicate that value effectively.
Africa has the diamonds.
Africa has the stories.
The opportunity now is to own both.
What do you believe will create the most value for natural diamonds in the next decade: origin storytelling, traceability, or local beneficiation?
#DiamondIndustry #NaturalDiamonds #DiamondWorld #DiamondTrade #DWMagazine

posts.caption:
On Wednesday, Trump-backed org Freedom 250 announced the lineup for the Great American State Fair, a concert series on the National Mall celebrating America’s 250th anniversary. It immediately got dragged online for being stacked with less relevant 90s acts and since then, multiple performers have bailed, saying the event was “misleading” and not the nonpartisan gig they were sold.
Morris Day & The Time were first to drop out, followed by Young MC, Martina McBride, The Commodores and Bret Michaels, spawning trending memes of fictitious bands also pulling out. On the other hand, Vanilla Ice is fully committed, posting a video that has sus sponcon energy for an event that is already falling apart.
Freedom Williams of C+C Music Factory posted his response in a 7-minute rant while sitting on a toilet and wearing a Hunger Games shirt. At first, he said he told his agent he was out, then pivoted to dragging Democrats who threatened to cancel him, and now says he might still show up for the “MAGA crew.”
Milli Vanilli is also messy. The group’s “original/real vocalists” issued a statement saying they wouldn’t perform and that “others using the name ‘Milli Vanilli’.. should be considered a tribute band with no association.” Meanwhile, Fab Morvan, the surviving lipsyncer, says he 𝘪𝘴 performing ¯\_(ツ)_/¯
Flo Rida is the only act booked who has yet to make a statement so far. A 2023 New Yorker piece profiled him as part of the billionaire-private-gig circuit, so the jury is still out… if the concerts happen at all.
For something just announced this week, it’s already looking like a shitshow. Sad!
#news #90s #music #concert #dc
posts.alt_text: Photo by Diet Prada ™ on May 29, 2026. May be a meme of text that says 'Olivia Rodrigo Claps Back at the Back Bab Babydoll Controversy On The New York Times's 'Popcast' podcast, the singer expressed her frustration for the response to her recent outfits. Diet PRADA D0'.

posts.caption:
For the last few weeks, the debate over Olivia Rodrigo’s babydoll dresses has been inescapable. The image of Rodrigo, performing ‘Drop Dead’ in Barcelona in a pink floral babydoll dress, conjured audience’s pent up fury about the Epstein Files, and they accused Rodrigo of sexualizing pedophilic imagery. On The New York Times’s pop-culture podcast ‘Popcast’, on which Rodrigo was recently a guest, the issue was referred to as a “lightning rod.”
“That’s been making me so upset,” Rodrigo responded.
“What does it mean to you?” asked Joe Coscarelli, the podcast’s host.
Rodrigo described risque outfits she’s worn in the past which people didn’t seem to take issue with — “a sparkly bra and, like, little shorts” — and explained how it “disturbed” her that people were, by contrast, bothered by her new, more feminine looks which cover her body more.
“It just shows how we really normalize pedophilia on our culture,” she said.
She went on to explain how women and girls should not be held at fault for the sexualization that men impose upon their bodies or their clothes, a stance that many of her supporters have also taken, and even cited the punk/grunge style icons who have, by association, also become key talking points in the Great Babydoll Debate of 2026.
“I was like, ‘this is so cool!’ I feel like Kathleen Hanna or like Courtney Love, all these people who are my heroes. And I felt cool and comfortable in it,” she said. “And I just think if we start dressing in a way that’s like, ‘oh, I don’t want some freak to think that I’m sexy like a baby’ or some crazy thing like that… I just think it’s losing the plot a little bit.”
And yet, Rodrigo’s defense of her outfits has not quelled the debate. Some continue to doubt Rodrigo’s agency in choosing her own looks while some believe that she is the one responsible for normalizing pedophilia. At the end of the day, here we are, yet again, debating what a young woman can and can’t wear, even when she has patiently explained why she is wearing it.
Has the plot officially been lost?

posts.caption:
Michael Preysman, the founder of Everlane, is launching a new fashion brand called ‘Still Radical’ in response to Everlane’s sale to Shein. While the product offering is unclear, the company is meant to bring back the “vision of Everlane but with a new take that meets the moment.”
Thus, the launch page for the as-yet-unnamed brand promises no private equity or venture capital involved in the business, which Preysman describes as “an experiment.” Perhaps then, if Everlane’s sustainability and transparency model represented the ‘millennial dream’ in the 2010s, Still Radical represents the same generation’s disenchantment with VC/PE; Where the dream used to be about organic fibers and responsible manufacturing, it is now about getting the capitalism out of capitalism.
The Shein sale, though, doesn’t inspire much confidence. Preysman, in a letter published by Vogue yesterday, claims that he didn’t know about the Shein sale until “20 minutes before,” and that the internet’s negative response to the sale comforted him as it demonstrated how consumers still crave sustainability and transparency. Over the weekend he “meditated” on the “ironic” Shein news before coming up with his radical new company idea.
“Today, the customer is looking for that hope again,” he writes. “The world has changed, and we’re in a period where capitalism is in full force. There isn’t quite the balance between capitalism and equity, and between capitalism and creativity.”
Silicon Valley startup fashion brands like Everlane haven’t been quick to learn the lessons that fashion industry brands have been learning for years: Fashion is volatile, unpredictable, moody. One day you’re in, and the next day you’re out! The business models that work for other consumer products don’t quite translate to fashion either. Hence, AllBirds, a Silicon Valley footwear brand, recently pivoted its business from shoes to AI.
Nevertheless, the tech world seems to view fashion as an extractable resource, one that comes with a measure of cultural clout which can’t be mined in other industries. Can they finally crack the code as a tech company that looks like a fashion brand? Or is it doomed from the start?

posts.caption:
Images of the White House lawn are going viral as construction for a UFC cage match on the site is underway (yes, really). The “UFC Freedom 250” event is slated for June 14, to coincide with the US’s 250th anniversary, Trump’s birthday and Flag Day.
“I’ve been involved in a lot of big events,” the president said about the fight last month. “I have never had an event that has had more interest than the UFC fight we have right at the front door.”
Trump first mentioned the idea last July 4 while talking about plans for the nation’s anniversary celebration. “Think of this on the grounds of the White House. We have a lot of land there,” he said. The statement came just one day after Vance was deployed to Pakistan for negotiations with officials in Tehran about six weeks into the war with Iran, per MS Now.
So to recap what’s happening at the White House complex: on one side, construction crews are building a ballroom where the East Wing once was. On the other, the Rose Garden has been paved over, replaced by a concrete patio. And now at the center, there’s a giant steel cage where fighters will punch each other in front of the president for his 80th birthday.
#idiocracy #news #reels #threads #washington

posts.caption:
We could go see Boop!
Quinta Brunson is set to develop and star as Betty Boop in a new film by her production company Fifth Chance Productions, alongside creator Max Fleischer’s grandson Mark Fleischer and Fleischer Studios.
According to the film’s press release, the story will trace Betty’s origin and evolution through Max Fleischer’s perspective, digging into the “relationship between the artist and his creation as he navigates the creative and commercial pressures of building one of the world’s first animated icons, particularly as that icon begins to take on a life of its own.”
Online reactions to Brunson stepping into the role are split. Some fans see the two-time Emmy winner as a perfect fit for the character’s reboot, while others are questioning her casting.
Betty Boop’s influence and origins have long-sparked debate. While Fleischer claimed to have based the character on a dog, singer Helen Kane famously sued the studio, arguing Boop was modeled after her. In court, the studio pointed the character’s iconic baby voice to Black performers of the 1920s, including Baby Esther, a popular child star.
Betty’s roots have been debatable for decades, but either way, she’s a fictional character and Brunson is a great choice for the legendary diva!
Swipe through for more of her fascinating history.
#bettyboop #quintabrunson #film #history #animation

posts.caption:
After years of soft neutrals, skin tints, and “no-makeup makeup” dominating the algorithm, Marc Jacobs Beauty is relaunching on its own terms with statement shades and party balloon-inspired packaging.
The brand first debuted in 2013 and quickly reached cult status before disappearing in 2021 after its partnership with Kendo ended. Now it returns under a new license with Coty Inc., re-entering a market that has fundamentally shifted.
While “clean girl” minimalism has dominated beauty since the brand shuttered, the cultural pendulum seems to be swinging towards color and maximalism, and away from former mega-brands like Glossier.
Marc Jacobs Beauty is returning with saturated pigments, high-gloss finishes, and sculptural packaging in a relaunch Jacobs calls a celebration of “creativity and self-expression.”
The rollout includes seven hero products priced from $26 to $42, reviving fan favorites like its best-selling gel liners in 21 shades, alongside new products like a multi-use blush stick.
Whether this return meaningfully disrupts the beauty category remains to be seen, but it’s clearly not trying to blend in.
Marc Jacobs Beauty drops May 28 on marcjacobs.com, followed by a June 1 release at Sephora.
#marcjacobs #beauty #makeup #marcjacobsbeauty #sephora

posts.caption:
Remember Anisha? “Meri agency, meri reputation” etc? What a role. @iamkitugidwani bodied it!!
Because of the advent of A.I. and everything slowly flattening into code, prompts, algorithms, dashboards, and optimisation-slop, culture is weirdly circling back to the oldest currency of all: taste. Actual taste. Scary! The intangible thing you cannot automate no matter how many Midjourney or Claude prompts you generate. Which is why the women in branding, fashion, media, luxury, beauty, production etc, all the so-called “marketing girlies”, are emerging as the final arbiters of culture. The gatekeepers of what’s in, what becomes aspirational, what becomes cringe, what enters the group chat, and more.
Brands now shape culture more than institutions do, and the people shaping those brands inevitably become cultural architects. Meanwhile the finance bros, growth hackers, optimisation kings, LinkedIn hustle philosophers lol, and low-octave voice-using male podcasters have decided they too are now “creative directors” because they discovered the words storytelling and community in a PPM deck.
So girlies, are y’all reclaiming the “marketing girly” aka women in SPAM or no?
posts.alt_text: #DSWEDDINGS Don’t we all love LOVE? Especially when it comes with floral arrangements, film cameras, Polki diamonds, and a cross-cultural collision of Marathi tradition & French romance (bonjuaaaa). @eelysp’s wedding to Gautier Falluel was a seamless merging of Maharashtrian ritual and French sensibility, where every detail felt thoughtfully curated. From the venue to the flowers and even the shela, everything looked like it could arrive with an accompanying exhibition note explaining its cultural significance. The same heartfelt “I do,” just dialled up with an impossibly refined artistic eye. It was essentially an art curator’s guide to the perfect Parisian ceremony. Forever and ever, without too much cheesiness hehe 🎀💅🥰

posts.caption: Desi Bling was deeply, deeply unsettling to watch. #ELLECoverStar: There is a version of Zinnia Kumar (now Rose) (@zinniakumar) that fashion understands easily. The model. An image that moves seamlessly between Paris, New York, and wherever the industry gathers next.
It is also the least interesting version of her.
We’re at Le Bristol in Paris and the day carries all the familiar markers of a luxury cover shoot. Controlled light. Precise framing. Cartier anchoring the narrative. But Rose doesn’t perform that script in the expected way. She arrives ready, slips into each look without negotiation, barely checks the monitor. There is no sense of overworking the image, only an instinctive understanding of how to move within it. Head to the 🔗 in bio to read our conversation with the cover star.
On @zinniakumar: Clash de Cartier earrings in rose gold and green dyed agate, Clash de Cartier necklace in rose gold, Clash de Cartier ring in rose gold and chalcedony, Clash De Cartier ring in rose gold and onyx, and Clash de Cartier ring in rose gold and red dyed agate, all by @cartier. Textured draped top paired and draped trousers both by @newbottega

#ELLECoverStar: Between takes, Zinnia Rose (@zinniakumar) speaks about working with domestic violence and coercive control survivors, particularly within South Asian communities. About helping women reclaim confidence, voice, and agency within legal systems that often fail to recognise the complexity of their experiences.
“I haven’t spoken about this publicly before, but I spent ten years in an abusive, financially and coercively controlling marriage,” she shares. “It shaped how much of myself I could express. Reclaiming myself and healing has been a long process, and one of the first things I did was remove the name he gave me. I’m returning to my birthright, as Zinnia Jothi Rose.”
Head to the 🔗 in bio to read our conversation with the cover star.
On @zinniakumar: Clash de Cartier earrings in rose gold and onyx, Clash de Cartier necklace in yellow gold and onyx, Clash de Cartier ring in yellow gold and onyx, and Clash de Cartier bracelet in yellow gold and onyx, all by @cartier. #ELLEShowTime: I've loved Holly Jackson's A Good Girl's Guide to Murder series for years, partly because it delivers genuinely addictive mysteries, but mostly because it centres girls who refuse to accept the stories they're handed. Pip Fitz-Amobi has always been my kind of protagonist: relentlessly curious, annoyingly persistent, and entirely unwilling to let powerful men get away with terrible things. In a world where girls are constantly told to stay quiet, Pip keeps asking questions. And honestly? I love a story where women get their revenge, expose the truth, and chip away at the systems designed to protect men. Head to the 🔗 in bio to read more.
Words by: @16ektasinha

posts.caption:
Presenting our first of four Summer Issue cover stars, @annehathaway.
Due to filming and release delays, Hathaway now has five movies premiering within six months. Her insanely busy year continues with her starring role as Penelope in @theodysseymovie. (To which she says: “It’s crazy to have four years of work coming out in six months.”) After weathering the storms of Hollywood, the in-demand star is finally collecting her flowers—and stopping to smell them. “If I’m not enjoying it, then really, really what’s wrong with me?” she tells ELLE. “If I can’t have a positive outlook on it when I’m having such a fortunate ride, then it would be wasted on me.”
For ELLE’s Summer 2026 issue, Hathaway reflects on how she’s grown since the early days of her career, reuniting with Christopher Nolan after 12 years, and navigating online criticism: “We’re at a time when people feel very confident in assuming what they think is fact.” Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
The statement you would hear the loudest at the 2026 #Cannes Film Festival was the fact that it was a relatively quiet year. Sure, some movies inspired old-fashioned bidding wars (like @jtfirstman's ‘Club Kid,’ which eventually went to A24), and international superstars like Demi Moore and Bella Hadid decorated the red carpets. But this year's edition was still one that was hampered by a so-so competition lineup—usually the festival’s most hotly anticipated segment—and a cinematic vibe of generally underwhelmed attendees.
Still, you get to choose your own adventure every time you attend a film festival, and Cannes is no exception. At the link in bio, find the 13 films from the Croisette ELLE thinks you should look forward to.

posts.caption:
If the French Open handed out trophies on fashion merits, @naomiosaka would have already won the tournament. The tennis star has taken to the historic clay of @rolandgarros in Paris in looks that channel the city’s storied couture savoir-faire for the court—or, as she aptly called it, “court-ure.”
“We wanted to design a world in and around the [Nike kit] to build more of a narrative,” Osaka’s creative collaborator @themartyharper tells ELLE. “We didn’t want the story to be told as soon as she walked out. Naomi always says fashion is an opportunity to let people see more of her.”
From sheer maxi skirts to sequined tennis dresses, Osaka's #FrenchOpen looks are already winners. At the link in bio, Harper opens up about bringing the tennis superstar's fashion narrative to life.

posts.caption:
For six and a half years on @bravo’s ‘Vanderpump Rules,’ @rachelleviss assumed the role of a pageant queen turned flighty waitress turned damsel in distress, before her final act, in season 10, as a homewrecker. Now, three years later, her life has taken a decidedly different turn.
As a certified sound bath practitioner, she is the maestro of a soft-girl orchestra, and glisteningly pearlescent singing bowls are her ensemble. The frenzied intoxication, dysregulated screaming matches, regrettable hookups, and performative dim-wittedness demanded by Leviss’s former job on television are worlds away from what she wants to offer now.
“I have a therapist who specializes in narcissistic abuse,” Leviss says. “And she helped me to view ‘Vanderpump Rules’ as a narcissist—the network and production are narcissistic. They will use you and get what they need out of you, and then discard you.”
At the link in bio, Leviss goes deep on her role in the #Scandoval mess, her mental health journey after it all, and her newfound career path.

Dhruv Vikram’s (@dhruv.vikram) road to his debut was anything but straightforward. What began as the Tamil remake of Arjun Reddy was completed, shelved over creative differences, and later reworked entirely with a new cast and direction. He is brief when asked about the setbacks before his debut. “It is brutal, right? You got to do what you've got to do to endure. There is no other choice,” is all he offers.

For Dhruv Vikram (@dhruv.vikram), the journey to the Lee Strasberg Theatre & Film Institute, where he was shipped off to learn his craft by his father, became the first real break from identity and expectation. “When I was in Chennai and said that I wanted to act, it was not accepted very easily. But when I went to New York, nobody cared about who my dad was. I was one amongst many people who were passionate about acting, which is what made it easier,” he says.

Now I’m trying to take it all to heart as I peruse my next collection
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by Shakaila Forbes-Bell on October 14, 2025 tagging @secondhand_pigeon, and @fashionispsychology.

posts.caption:
“Hi Shakaila we’re xyz producers and we’d love to make a tv show with you” I’ve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from 🤩 to 🫩
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that’s what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself 😅
This black history month especially, I’m inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it’s possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That’s why I’m so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover

As she continues to soar to new heights, Pooja’s achievement serves as an inspiration for aspiring athletes across the country.

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠
Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠
posts.alt_text: Photo by Good On You: sustainability ratings on January 21, 2026.

posts.caption:
Meet the top rated brands of 2025 🎊⁠
⁠
In the past year, we rated more than 1500 brands and are proud to share the ten that received our highest marks, illustrating a commitment to more sustainable practices that protect people, the planet, and animals 💚⁠
⁠
What’s a theme that unites these brands? 👀 Outside of a commitment to reducing their impact, these brands are all small and independent, a reminder of where to look in the industry for leadership and innovation. If you’re looking for a reminder to support small brands this year, this is it.⁠
⁠
Cheers to all these brands, along with all the brands that were rated “Good” and “Great” in the past year! Here’s to continuing to make a difference and for championing a better approach to what we wear. Find the full list at the link—and share which brands you’re hoping to see rated in 2026 in the comments 💬⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #SustainableBeauty #EcoBeauty #Beauty #BeautyBrand #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion

posts.caption:
What can be accomplished in ten years?⁠
⁠
A decade may seem short but, in reflecting on all the work we’ve done in the past decade, we’re in awe of everything that has transpired—and are grateful for the hard work of the current and former team members along with you.⁠
⁠
Whether you’ve shared our posts or have been a partner in this business, the work of creating a more sustainable future isn’t done alone but in partnership with others. These accomplishments are an example of what happens when likeminded people come together in the hopes of a better world 🌍🌎🌏⁠
⁠
Swipe to learn about ten things Good On You has accomplished in the past ten years. Share how you think the sustainable fashion and beauty industry have shifted in the past decade 💚⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
Just because you’re going to a music festival doesn’t mean you leave your values at home 🪶⁠
⁠
For years, fast fashion—and most big brands—have been pushing themselves into the conversation of festivals, monopolizing attention in the hopes that you buy, buy, buy what they’re selling. Don’t fall for it ⛔⁠
⁠
Instead, use this as an opportunity to flex your commitment to sustainability, by sharing clothes and wearing what you have. In need of something specific? This is where “Good” and “Great” brands come in, which are guaranteed to have better values while typically being small and independent.⁠
⁠
Swipe for an explainer on how fast fashion preys on festival fashion before exploring a full guide at the link ↑⁠
⁠
#FestivalStyle #MusicFestivals #MusicFestivalFashion #MusicFestivalStyle #Fashion⁠

posts.caption:
Bom dia, world! Portugal is a country to watch when it comes to sustainable fashion 🇵🇹⁠
⁠
With vegan footwear from @naeveganshoes to swimwear created using ocean waste from @conscious_thelabel, there is a diversity of exciting Portuguese brands working to rethink how a fashion brand can work 💚⁠
⁠
Swipe to meet a few of them, before visiting the link for the full list and for more brands from around the world to keep in mind 🔎 Which brand would you like to see rated next? Let us know in the comments ⏬⁠
⁠
#Portugal #PortugalFashion #PortugalStyle #Lisbon #StyleInspo⁠
posts.alt_text: Photo by Good On You: sustainability ratings on January 21, 2026.

posts.caption:
What *is* viscose anyway? And is it actually sustainable? 💭⁠
⁠
The answer is complicated, as it represents one of many materials made from man-made cellulosic fibre that is made from trees…but that doesn’t mean it’s better for the environment 😬⁠
⁠
Viscose is a reminder to always look into the impact of materials, to better understand what goes into its production and how that affects workers, environments, and animals 🌄⁠
⁠
Curious about alternatives to viscose and brands that are considering these alternatives? Visit the link, where we have a full list of brands prioritising lower-impact materials 🪡⁠
⁠
#Fashion #Sustainability #SustainableFashion #Zara #SHEIN

posts.caption:
Singer #CMAT has responded to body-shaming abuse she received after performing at BBC Radio 1’s Big Weekend.
‘There is no relief from this – nobody can protect me or save me from this, and all that is demanded of me is more and more work as every environment I am placed in becomes more hostile.’
She added: ‘I also want to point out, to some very well-meaning people, that I am not being defiant. I am not choosing to look like this or weigh this much as some kind of punk rock act of liberty. I simply have a body, one that I would of course like to change in order to fit in and avoid all of this abuse, but I have had extreme difficulty in doing so. I don’t get a say in whether or not I want to be brave, I simply have to sit here and take it.’
posts.alt_text: Photo by Grazia UK on May 29, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, magazine and text that says 'I 'It is literally so boring for me, a gorgeous genius, to keep having to yap on about how horribly I am treated because of my body. CMAT GRAZIA'.

And as for archival looks that were iconic but super fragile, like a sheer Prada top that seemed to be made of holy dust? posts.caption: If there’s an art to exposing the absurdities in everyday life, then @davidsedarisbooks is one of its greatest living practitioners. At the link in our bio, the author opens up to Bazaar's @kaitlyn_greenidge_author about finding humor in the chaos of new places and why understanding yourself often requires an audience.

posts.caption:
Just in time for football's biggest spectacle, @adidas and @cocacola are tapping into the energy, nostalgia, and global fandom that make the World Cup more than just a tournament. ⁠
⁠
The collection features a lineup of track tops, jerseys, shorts, graphic t-shirts, and a bright red Airliner bag that feels destined for away days and airport terminals alike.⁠
⁠
On the footwear front, adidas digs deep into its archive, bringing together the Adistar Control 5, Predator Sala, Climacool 1, and Megaride F50. The result is a collection that captures the spirit of football's biggest stage. It's slated to drop on June 6 via adidas.
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by Highsnobiety Style #HSStyle on May 29, 2026 tagging @thenorthface, @adamnaples, and @gucci_pineapple. May be an image of fishing, fish, fishing rod, outdoors and text that says 'A DAY IN THE HIKE WITH THE NORTH FACE H'.

posts.caption:
For @thenorthface, it’s always been more than a hike.
Pairing up musician and internet personality @gucci_pineapple with photographer and avid fisherman @adamnaples, the iconic outdoor brand supports the unlikely pair as they hiked along Esopus Creek.
Sporting the brand’s versatile outdoor gear designed to carry them through their trek, the duo cast out a few lines and created memories that will last longer than their afternoon excursion.
Click the link in bio for more.
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by Highsnobiety Style #HSStyle on May 30, 2026 tagging @adidas, and @cocacola. May be an image of magazine, poster, sportswear and text.

posts.caption:
Ahead of the 2026 World Cup, @424inc founder @guillermoandrade is doubling down on football-inspired leatherwear. Following the brand’s viral David Beckham jacket and its standout R9 Brazil leather from 424’s Paris debut, Andrade has now crafted a series of custom jackets for Germany’s Toni Rudiger, revealed exclusively by Highsnobiety.⁠
⁠
Rudiger arrived at Germany’s World Cup training camp wearing unreleased 424 x Under Armour pieces alongside two bespoke leather jackets: one featuring Germany’s national colors across the sleeves and another stamped with a fictional 424 x Under Armour football crest. Teammate Malick Thiaw also received a custom version. For now, the jackets remain exclusive to a select group of footballers, but 424’s upcoming Paris Fashion Week presentation will offer a closer look at the collaboration before its official release next year.
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by Highsnobiety Style #HSStyle on May 30, 2026 tagging @adidas, and @cocacola. May be an image of magazine, poster, sportswear and text.

posts.caption:
@newbottega has expanded its signature “TOSCA” series with a new wearable AirPods case and keychain, both crafted in the brand’s signature paper calf intreccio.⁠
⁠
Made to fit AirPods Pro, the case comes with a hand-woven leather strap that makes carrying them around feel a whole lot more luxe. ⁠
⁠
Both pieces are available now.
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by Highsnobiety Style #HSStyle on May 30, 2026 tagging @adidas, and @cocacola. May be an image of magazine, poster, sportswear and text.

posts.caption:
More than 20 years after its original debut, @oakley has reissued the legendary FatCat as part of its MUZM collection. A cult favorite from the early 2000s, the updated FatCat stays true to the original design with its organic silhouette and deeply set lenses, while introducing Oakley’s Prizm lens technology.⁠
⁠
The reissue also features lightweight O-Matter material across the frames and temples, delivering improved durability and flexibility. ⁠
⁠
Available in cream and matte black.
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by Highsnobiety Style #HSStyle on May 30, 2026 tagging @adidas, and @cocacola. May be an image of magazine, poster, sportswear and text.

posts.caption:
If you’ve ever wandered through a secondhand shop wondering who’s buying all that forgotten tat, the answer is apparently Miuccia Prada. @miumiu annual Upcycled capsule returns with another masterclass in turning vintage castoffs into covetable fashion, this time starting with the simplest of staples: white cotton shirts and khaki chinos. The result is a wardrobe of reconstructed pieces that feels unmistakably Miu Miu, complete with raw hems, floral embroidery, bows, ribbons, and slashed seams, all rendered in a utilitarian palette of black, white, and beige.⁠
⁠
This year’s collection sees trousers transformed into skirts, shirts into dresses and apron tops, while reconstructed khaki backpacks pair with matching Plume sneakers finished with customizable charms and laces. Modeled by Suki Waterhouse, the latest Upcycled drop continues Miu Miu’s ongoing remix of fashion’s leftovers into something entirely new, following previous collections that reworked denim, leather jackets, and Levi’s. In an industry overflowing with newly made clothes, Miu Miu Upcycled proves that with enough creativity and craftsmanship, everything old can become desirable again.⁠

posts.caption:
@juliawolfnyc is the alt-rock princess taking confessional lyricism to new heights and casting spells on the side. Carving out her own lane entirely, one filled with emotional spirals, TV references and brutally honest storytelling, we sat down with the artist ahead of her latest single release, “Deep End,” out now.
The self-proclaimed “sensitive girl” has mastered the art of turning her deepest anxieties and emotions into haunting lyrics that say the things most people only wish they could articulate.
After touring with @machinegunkelly, featuring on a @champagnepapi track and being sampled by @fakemink, Wolf now enters her next era. We sat down with the artist to discuss her latest track, future manifestations and even the possibility of a country-inspired release somewhere down the line. Head to the link in bio for the full interview and check out the new release.
Credits: @a_damp_owl

posts.caption:
@zenithwatches’ G.F.J. collection is built around one of the most celebrated movements in the brand’s history.
During a recent visit to the Swiss manufacture in Le Locle, we took a closer look at how the brand brought the legendary Calibre 135 back into the conversation. Originally developed for observatory chronometry competitions, the movement helped Zenith earn part of its record-setting 2,333 precision awards and remains one of the most important calibres in its archive.
From brick-inspired design details borrowed from the manufacture itself to a renewed focus on manual winding, the G.F.J. blends heritage and modern watchmaking in equal measure.
Photo/Video: Zoe Leung/Hypebeast
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by HYPEBEAST on April 28, 2026 tagging @hypetrak, @dontoliver, @hbx, and @hypebeastmag. May be an image of magazine, poster and text.

posts.caption:
@gantri is officially cutting the cord so you glow anywhere you go ⁠⚡️⁠
To celebrate the lighting innovator’s 10-year anniversary, the California-based brand is introducing Gantri Wireless, a sleek 10-piece collection created in collaboration with design studio @ammunitiongroup.⁠
⁠
Gantri “Helia” is the key innovation here; these lamps deliver up to 1000 lumens of brightness and up to 10 hours of battery life without messy cords. The drop features three distinct executions, each with its own character: Drift, Pier, and Eave. The brand is also debuting five new nature-inspired colorways, including an electric yellow Lichen and a burgundy red Manzanita.⁠
⁠
Staying close to their conscious design roots, every piece is still made on demand in San Leandro, California, using the company’s proprietary sugarcane-derived Gantri PlantPolymers.⁠ 🪴⁠
⁠
The Gantri Wireless collection is available for pre-order starting at $248, with shipments arriving in Fall 2026. ⁠
⁠
Photo: Gantri

posts.caption:
There are people who wear time, and then there are people who define it. @palaktiwarii belongs to the second kind.✨
She carries something rare, a weight that isn’t borrowed from the past, but isn’t chasing the future either. It simply exists, with the kind of quiet confidence that doesn’t need to announce itself.
In this frame, legacy and the now collapse into each other. @guesswatches on her wrist, intent in her eyes, highlighting the “Main Character” hunger behind the gold hardware; this is a story of a woman who isn’t just wearing the accessories of success, but is actively forging them. ⌚️
The Architecture of Time isn’t a concept. It’s a posture. A way of owning every second without letting a single one own you.
@palaktiwarii doesn’t follow the tempo. She sets it. And right now, she’s setting it for all of us. 🫶🏻

posts.caption:
@umathurman Chooses Happiness: For decades, Thurman has embodied a certain kind of unstoppable presence, and  this month, the icon returns to the grisly genre that cemented her stardom, as a bloodthirsty avenger in #PrettyLethal. But off-screen things are less grim: Thurman is energized by her work and looking forward to the possibilities of her next chapter—one where she’s making space for new roles, new interests, and more time for herself: “I think the choice to be happy is one that should be made regardless of any conditions.”
Read more now at the link in @instylemagazine’s bio.

posts.caption:
Kritika Kamra (@kkamra) and Gaurav Kapur (@gauravkaps) have been household names for years. He’s the OG VJ-turned-sports anchor and host of Breakfast with Champions; she went from primetime TV to silver screens and streaming favourites. Lately, though, the internet can’t stop talking about their marriage.
“The first thing anybody says to me now is, ‘How’s married life?’ And I want to say, it’s all the same. Except now I live with my best friend, and I don’t have to decide what’s for lunch every day.” says Kamra.
For their first-ever cover shoot post marriage, Kritika and Gaurav take Lifestyle Asia India on a little getaway with The Honeymooners.

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It’s been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‘Almost Famous,’ and she’s been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success—and her roots in Hollywood royalty—she’s remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
“I just really want to make movies that people love,” Hudson tells L’OFFICIEL. “It’s really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.”
That, of course, has always been Hudson’s strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‘Song Sung Blue,’ she’s now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‘Running Point.’ In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson’s own upbringing, she’s quick to dismiss that narrative. “Jeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,” she says. “For me, we’re just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they’re not.”
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
«Ci sono persone davvero brave in determinate cose, e tutte insieme funzionano in modo brillante, ma non tutti possono essere eccellenti in tutto. Negli anni ho imparato a capire cosa ti motiva e in che modo puoi essere utile a un progetto, soprattutto quando lavori in collaborazione», dichiara @KateHudson nella sua intervista con L’OFFICIEL.
«Se metti insieme persone di cui ti fidi e che sono brave in quello che fanno, ottieni la migliore esperienza possibile. Quando un ambiente di lavoro è instabile o caotico, raramente porta al successo».

posts.caption:
@KateHudson has taken on a wide range of genres, but she’s still focused on pushing herself even further. When we asked Hudson what she still hopes to achieve in her career, she didn’t point to accolades—just a desire to keep pursuing roles that scare her, the same approach she brings to everything she does. ⁠
⁠
“I enter every experience like it’s new, like I don’t know how it’s going to unfold,” she explains. “I come in doing my part, which is understanding what makes me feel confident in the role and the character, but I do that so that when I’m there, I can be flexible, not rigid.” Read Hudson’s full interview at the link in our bio. ⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
The real main character of ‘Euphoria?’ Maddy Perez’s closet.
As the ‘Euphoria’ series finale airs this Sunday, we’re revisiting some of Maddy’s (@alexademie) most iconic looks from this season. From the @versace Spring/Summer 2002 leather lace-up jacket to the @jeanpaulgaultier Fall/Winter 1995 orange fur-trimmed coat, and the @roberto_cavalli Spring/Summer 2003 dress, Maddy’s collection of archival designer pieces remains unmatched.
Some people are mourning the end of ‘Euphoria.’ We’re mourning the end of Maddy Perez’s outfits.
Photos courtesy of HBO Max
#euphoriahbo #euphoria #maddyperez #alexademie

posts.caption:
Fashion may change with the tide, but swimwear tells the story. From the elegance of postwar seaside dressing to the glamour of the jet-set years, the L’OFFICIEL archives reveal how swimwear has reflected changing ideals of beauty, leisure, and luxury. As #MiamiSwimWeek returns, we look back at the silhouettes that shaped summers past—and the cultural moments that shaped them.
Beyond the beach, these images offer a snapshot of how fashion has interpreted escape across generations. Whether defined by Riviera refinement, tropical exuberance, or unapologetic glamour, each era brought its own vision of the perfect getaway. Together, they chart the evolution of resort dressing as both fantasy and cultural artifact, reminding us that what we wear on holiday often says as much about the moment as it does the destination.
Photos courtesy of the L’OFFICIEL Archive

posts.caption:
⁠Diamonds may be a girl’s best friend, but for @SophieBilleBrahe they are something even more. “There’s something so magical about a diamond,” she tells L’OFFICIEL. “I always feel that they are borrowed; we’re passing through their lives.”⁠
⁠
After earning her master’s degree from London’s Royal College of Art, Sophie Bille Brahe returned to Denmark in 2011 to launch her eponymous jewelry label, positioning it at the intersection of craft, art, and fashion. Nature remains her greatest source of inspiration, especially the horizon where the sky meets the sea—a vision reflected in the wavy diamond and bezel setting of her signature Grand Ensemble Ocean ring.⁠
⁠
The way her jewels are displayed is equally important to Brahe, who even designed the tables for her Copenhagen store. “Jewelry is meant to be worn, and it dies a little bit when it’s just lying somewhere,” she says. “I thought, ‘how do you give something so small the right attention in a space?’ I wanted it to stand out but still represent me, and look as clean as possible.”⁠
⁠
Brahe, who prioritizes memory and inherited objects over decoration, fills her spaces with family heirlooms tied to her aristocratic past. For her New York boutique, she installed 19th-century Bertel Thorvaldsen lion sculptures from her family’s former estate—what she calls the most precious pieces in her archive, saying, “If I can’t be there every day to take care of this place, at least the lions are there to guard it.”⁠
⁠
⁠
For our May issue, Brahe opened up her archive to L’OFFICIEL, revealing the inspirations behind her creations at the link in our bio.⁠
⁠
Writer @samanthajsimon⁠
Photos courtesy of Sophie Bille Brahe ⁠
⁠
#SophieBilleBrahe

posts.caption:
@Balenciaga dropped its Spring Summer 2026 preview collection in Mumbai last night, and the energy in the room felt different.
With Fatima Sana Sheikh, Bobby Deol, Mouni Roy, Sanya Malhotra, Malavika Mohanan, and Krithi Shetty in attendance, it felt less like a formal showcase and more like a real moment around fashion. The room was sharp, observant, and engaged.
Fatima Sana Shaikh said it best: “Balenciaga always has a chic approach and the brand is now heading towards it even more aggressively.” And that tone carried through the evening. Nothing felt loud for attention, yet everything commanded it.
What stood out was how people responded. There was curiosity, closer inspection, and genuine interest in the details. Some silhouettes felt familiar but more refined, while others brought a quiet sense of drama without trying too hard.
That is when a collection works.
Not when it demands attention, but when it earns it.
@balenciaga #Balenciaga

posts.caption:
Jacob & Co. continues to blur the line between watchmaking, sculpture, and fantasy with the Astronomia “Gates of Olympus.”
Priced at $1.4 million, the one-of-one creation draws inspiration from Greek mythology, featuring Zeus, Olympus, celestial movement, and a fully mechanical miniature world built directly into the timepiece itself.
Inside the 18K rose gold case sits a triple-axis tourbillon, over 9 carats of baguette diamonds, and an intricate Olympus scene designed as part of the movement.
More than a watch, the piece reflects how modern haute horology is increasingly moving toward collectible art and immersive storytelling.
@JacobAndCo #JacobAndCo #Luxurious #Collab

Alex Gonzalez recently showcased one of the most unusual watches ever created: the Jacob & Co. Astronomia Casino.
Crafted in rose gold and white gold, the timepiece features a fully functional miniature roulette wheel integrated into its movement, combining high jewellery, mechanical engineering, and artistic design in a way few watches can match.
More than a watch, the Astronomia Casino reflects Jacob & Co.’s reputation for pushing the boundaries of modern horology through bold and unconventional creations.
@jacobandco

posts.caption:
Louis Vuitton has created a one-of-one luxury timepiece to celebrate a decade of its partnership with UNICEF.
Called the Unity Time Object, the sculptural clock reimagines a football through high horology, featuring a skeletonised movement developed with Swiss clockmaker L’Épée 1839, 264 diamonds, and a bespoke Monogram trophy trunk. The unique piece will be auctioned through Sotheby’s, with proceeds supporting UNICEF’s global humanitarian programs.
More than a collectible, the project reflects how luxury brands are increasingly blending craftsmanship, philanthropy, and cultural storytelling into one-off creations designed far beyond traditional retail.
📸: @louisvuitton

posts.caption:
Hermès has turned the DJ booth into a luxury collectible.
Created through the maison’s bespoke Atelier Horizons division, the new Disque Jockey Club is a fully functional DJ setup crafted from mahogany wood, cowhide leather, and premium Japanese turntables. Designed with British DJ Prince Charles, the piece blends high-fidelity audio with Hermès craftsmanship, transforming music equipment into furniture-grade luxury.
More than just a sound system, the project reflects how luxury brands are increasingly expanding into design, lifestyle, and home culture, where even a DJ console can become a statement piece.
Images: @hermes

#ManifestCover
In Manifest’s May-June cover interview, Rukmini Vasanth (@rukmini_vasanth) reflects on grief, love, and the kind of resilience that quietly reshapes a person over time. Beneath the poise is a life shaped by movement, loss, and emotional inheritance, experiences that continue to inform the way she understands connection and presence.
“You don’t know what life will throw at you. But as long as you’re there for each other, that’s what matters. It’s about support and presence more than anything else,” she shares.
Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story from the link in bio.

posts.alt_text:
#ManifestCover

In motion, Rukmini Vasanth (@rukmini_vasanth) feels most like herself. Unhurried, intuitive, and quietly grounded.

Away from the noise, she leans into what steadies her. “A perfect day off is restful, restorative, and quietly grounding,” she shares in her cover interview with us.

That sense of stillness carries through everything she does. Even her understanding of love is simple, shaped by what she has seen up close. “You don’t know what life will throw at you. But as long as you’re there for each other, that’s what matters.”

Between movement and pause, she holds on to what is real.

Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story from the link in bio.
